title: Functions & Variables
overview: Every Python program is built from two fundamental ideas: variables that store data, and functions that perform actions. In this lecture you'll learn what they are, how to use them, and why they make programming so powerful.

blocks.title: In This Lecture You Will Learn
blocks.body: Welcome to the very beginning of programming with Python. By the end of this lecture you'll understand the two core building blocks of every program.
bullets: What variables are and how they store values
How to create and name variables in Python
What functions are and why they are useful
How to define your own functions with def
How to use the built-in print() and input() functions
How to format strings using f-strings

blocks.content: Programming is about telling a computer what to do, step by step. Variables give us a way to remember information, and functions give us a way to organize our instructions into reusable blocks. Let's explore both.

blocks.title: What is a Variable?
blocks.body: A **variable** is a named container that stores a value in your program. You give it a name and assign it a value using the `=` operator. You can then use that name anywhere in your code to access the stored value.

blocks.title: Think of a Variable as a Labeled Storage Box
blocks.text: Imagine a physical storage box with a label on it. The label is the variable name. What's inside the box is the value. You can open the box to look inside, replace what's stored, or use its contents in a recipe — all by referring to the label.

leftPanel.label: Real Life Analogy
leftPanel.title: A Labeled Storage Box
leftPanel.description: You put something inside and give it a name so you can find it later.

blocks.title: Variable Rules
blocks.body: Python variable names must follow a few simple rules.
bullets: Start with a letter or underscore (not a number)
Contain only letters, numbers, and underscores
Are case-sensitive: name and Name are different variables
Cannot be a Python keyword like print, if, or def

blocks.title: Creating and Using Variables
blocks.code:
name = "Lakshanya"
age = 20
height = 5.6

print(name)
print(age)
print(height)

blocks.title: Updating a Variable
blocks.code:
count = 0
print(count)

count = count + 1
print(count)

count = count + 1
print(count)

blocks.question: What do you think will be printed if we change the value of `name` to "John" before calling print()?
blocks.answer: It will print John instead of Lakshanya. When you reassign a variable, the old value is discarded and the new value takes its place. The box is emptied and refilled.

blocks.title: Store Your Name
blocks.instructions: Create a variable called name and assign your name to it. Then print a greeting using that variable.
blocks.starterCode:
# Create a variable with your name
# Then print: Hello, [name]!
blocks.hint: Use name = "YourName" to create the variable. Then use print(f"Hello, {name}!") to display a greeting.

blocks.title: What is a Function?
blocks.body: A **function** is a named block of code that performs a specific task. You define it once using `def`, and then call it by name whenever you need it. Functions help you avoid repeating the same code over and over.

blocks.title: Think of a Function as a Recipe
blocks.text: A recipe is a set of steps written once that you can follow many times to make the same dish. You don't rewrite the recipe each time — you just say 'make pasta' and follow the steps. Functions work exactly the same way.

leftPanel.label: Real Life Analogy
leftPanel.title: A Recipe Book
leftPanel.description: A recipe is a set of steps you write once and follow many times — just like a function.

blocks.title: Anatomy of a Function
blocks.body: A function has three parts: a **name**, optional **parameters** (inputs), and a **body** (the code that runs).
bullets: def — the keyword that starts a function definition
name — what you call to run the function
body — the indented code that runs when called
return — optionally sends a value back

blocks.title: A Function That Returns a Value
blocks.code:
def add(a, b):
    return a + b

result = add(3, 4)
print(result)

blocks.question: What happens if we call greet("Lakshanya") three times in a row?
blocks.answer: Each call runs the function body again. You get the greeting printed three times — this is exactly why functions are useful! Write the code once, run it as many times as you need.
blocks.followupCode:
def greet(name):
    print(f"Hello, {name}!")

greet("Lakshanya")
greet("Lakshanya")
greet("Lakshanya")
blocks.followupOutput:
Hello, Lakshanya!
Hello, Lakshanya!
Hello, Lakshanya!

blocks.title: Write a Greeting Function
blocks.instructions: Define a function called say_hello that takes a name parameter and prints a greeting. Then call it twice with different names.
blocks.starterCode:
# Define say_hello(name) here

# Call it twice
blocks.hint: Use def say_hello(name): on the first line, then indent the print statement. Don't forget to call the function after defining it.

blocks.title: Formatting Strings with f-Strings
blocks.body: An **f-string** (formatted string literal) lets you embed variable values directly inside a string. Prefix your string with `f` and wrap variables in curly braces `{}`.
bullets: Prefix the string with the letter f
Python replaces {name} with the value of name
You can put any expression inside the braces

blocks.title: f-String Examples
blocks.code:
name = "Lakshanya"
age = 20

print(f"My name is {name}")
print(f"I am {age} years old")
print(f"Next year I will be {age + 1}")
blocks.expectedOutput:
My name is Lakshanya
I am 20 years old
Next year I will be 21

blocks.title: Build a Profile
blocks.instructions: Create two variables: name (your name) and city (your city). Then use an f-string to print: "[name] lives in [city]."
blocks.hint: Use f"{name} lives in {city}." inside print(). Make sure your variables have values assigned first.

keyTakeaways.text: Variables store values using the = operator
Use = for assignment (not == which checks equality)
Functions package code for reuse with def
Functions can be called as many times as needed
f-strings embed variable values in text with {}
Variables must be defined before you use them

whatYouLearned: How to create a variable and assign it a value
How to update a variable's value
How to define a function with def
How to pass parameters to a function
How to return a value from a function
How to use f-strings for string formatting
